text: The surgeon begins addressing subsurface hematomas from the lower part of the sinus. Pericardial fat is removed from the pericardial surface using an harmonic scalpel, which precisely cuts and coagulates the tissue.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to perform fine dissection, carefully separating the lung from the surface of the tumor, which is tightly adhered. The lung parenchyma is preserved as the dissection reveals no invasion into the lung tissue.

The surgeon visualizes the entire course of the left phrenic nerve within the subcyphoidal pouch, highlighting the clear identification and manipulation of the nerve. The subcyphoid approach allows for complete visualization of the sternal notch and dissection of the left cervical pole up to the thyroid gland level using a harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon uses robotic seizures to open the pericardial space, providing a clear view of the cardiac structures. The three-dimensional vision of the robotic system aids in avoiding injury during this complex procedure.

The surgeon opens the pericardium beneath the phrenic nerve, revealing the infiltrating tumor inside the pericardial space. The tumor closely abuts the right phrenic nerve, but the nerve is preserved through fine dissection using robotic seizures.

The surgeon uses the free articulation and fine control of robotic arms to separate the tumor tightly abutting the superior vena cava (SVC) within the pericardial space. The robotic system aids in finding a safe dissection plane inside the pericardium, where the cancer infiltrates surrounding tissue.

A branch from the superior vena cava (SVC) is clipped and divided. The final view of the operation shows both phrenic nerves clearly visualized. The entire pericardium has been removed between the tumor and the heart, and the pericardial defect is covered with a cortex membrane patch.
